speaker: interviewee
text: I'm crawling through the undergrowth, and I'm challenged by a guard. So sheepishly, I get up. He says, [GERMAN]? And to my amazement, the guard is in SS uniform, the kapo from the kleiderkammer in Auschwitz. This man, you know, with the left hand. And he's got a sub-machine gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to me, Michael, I almost shot you. What you doing here? So I say to him, what are you doing here?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So he said, to get out of the camp, I volunteered for the SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to him, well, I'm running away. I mean, I knew him very well. You know? He was like a soldier-- he was the sugar daddy who permitted the whole thing to start with, in-- in Auschwitz. So he says, you don't have to run, because you'll be free within a couple of days. The war's over. So I said, but I'm dying of hunger and thirst. So he says, I've got nothing on me. And I can't go back to the guard house. And we could see the guard house now. I mean, I didn't even know that I was actually crawling towards the guard house. You understand?

speaker: interviewer
text: You were going in the wrong direction.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was going in completely the wrong direction. So he says, come tomorrow. I'll bring you some food. So I've got a whole day. But now I know the direction in which to crawl. He's going to be there and bring some food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I go there. And there's no one about. So I go nearer the guardhouse. And there's no one-- no one about.

speaker: interviewee
text: I walk into the guardhouse, and it's empty. Papers strewn all over, bits of clothing. It's empty.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was afraid to put the light on. It's dark, you see-- middle of the forest. I thought to myself, my god, the SS is gone. We're not being guarded. So I go back into the camp, and the first fellow I meet is Hunzer Polak. He's on that picture there. And I say, [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: We called him by a nickname called [NON-ENGLISH]. Because he once said, during a blackout in Theresienstadt, he once called out, [NON-ENGLISH], which means, you put out the light. And he said it so quickly and in such a frightened voice, that it sounded like [NON-ENGLISH] instead of [NON-ENGLISH]. So we called him [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, [NON-ENGLISH], the guards are gone. We're free. And we walked out. Night time. And we walk about a half mile along the field. And there are five Americans on a platoon, [NON-ENGLISH]. And [NON-ENGLISH] spoke some English, because he'd gone to a school in Prague. So we talked to these American soldiers. And we say to them, have you got some food?

speaker: interviewee
text: All they had was chewing gum. Neither of us had seen chewing gum. They give us chewing gum. So we chewed it and swallowed it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were-- and then they said, how is it that [NON-ENGLISH] looks so emaciated and you look all right? You know, I was still fat from Auschwitz, although it was, by now, May. So I had January, February, March, April, May, 5 months of real hard privation.

speaker: interviewee
text: But [NON-ENGLISH] had a whole year. So he was like a skeleton. And I was tolerably all right. I mean, I wasn't fat. I was thin. But I wasn't-- I wasn't emaciated.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said to him, it's-- it's a long story. You know? So where can we find some food? So they said that they came from a railway yard where there was an abandoned Panzer train.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, a train with German tanks. And perhaps in this train we will find some food amongst the rations. They said, go to the railway yard. You'll find some food there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we go on. And they said, you know, we don't know where the front is. We're just a party reconnoitering. And they asked, you know, where the camp was. And we told them. And we said, there's more than 2,000 people there. You know? And they have got no food. Many of them are ill, terrible overcrowding.

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't too interested. Except they wanted to know where it was. They didn't want to go there. We wanted to take them back. But they said, no. You go and find your food. And they wanted to do their-- they wanted to do their recce.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we get to this Panzer train. And you see tanks loaded on flat cars, and then pass wagons, you know, for personnel. We search.

speaker: interviewee
text: We get to a place where there are provisions. And we find cans. Sort of big cans like this, tall ones. We don't know what's in there. Unmarked. You know we think some sort of food. And there was every kind of armament on this train. You know, from tanks to guns, machine guns. But in the whole train, there wasn't a single tin opener. [LAUGHS] We eventually found a bayonet. And we opened this tin. And it was bully beef, German bully beef. It was all fat, you know, cold. We had no way of heating it up. But we [? et ?] it. Tasted horrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next morning, we see the road is completely full of transport, you know? Army transport. And that was, in fact, the Americans already. So we get on the road and we start hitchhiking. And [NON-ENGLISH], we know where we are. [NON-ENGLISH]-- Where were you trying to get to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] had an aunt living in a place called Pisek, which is in Southern Bohemia. Which must be about 70 kilometers from Mauthausen. And we knew where Mauthausen was because we had walked that. And we knew the Danube. We had to cross the Danube. So we were going to Pisek, to this aunt. We get on the main road and we start hitchhiking. And one of these articulated lorries comes up. A man stops, American soldiers. He speaks a little bit of German. He takes us in his cab. And he looks at [NON-ENGLISH], and he says-- he sort of puts his hand on his forehead. He says, you've got a fever. And he drives us to a place called Wels, where there's an airfield. And he turns into the airfield. And he says, look I'll take you straight to hospital. You can go whenever you want. But you are obviously ill. You've got to go, since there might be something wrong with you as well, looking at me. I was still all right. And I thought we were-- we had food poisoning from this tin of meat. So I told him about the tin of meat. You know, the tin of bully beef. But in fact, it was typhus.

speaker: interviewee
text: So since I was all right, I said to [NON-ENGLISH], look, I'll go to your aunt. I'll remember the address. I'll tell her you're alive. And you follow. And I then had a very adventurous several days before I fell ill, also with typhus. But it's-- the-- the-- it-- I don't understand how these events follow-- followed me about. You know? Like, I get to Linz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other side of the Danube is the Russian zone. So the Americans have a way of registering you if you're a returnee. And you can go to a sort of clearing camp in the town of Linz, where there are other Jews. And you can have a bath. And you can get food. And I wanted shoes. Because my shoes had disappeared somewhere. Forgot where. And they say, the only-- the Russians won't let you across. Because with the Russians, you have to go back to Mauthausen. And I said, no. Whatever happens, I don't want to go to Mauthausen. I'm going back to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they said, well, Czechoslovakia is to the north. You have to go through the Russian zone. So eventually, one of them explains to me that you have to walk by the IG Farben Factory, which is on one side of Linz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when you walk along the railway near the IG Farben Factory, you get out to a railway bridge. And there's a Russian soldier there who lets people across without a pass. I don't know why. And they knew about it. It was a long walk. It took me like, half a day to walk. I mean, Linz is a big town. And I get to this bridge. And I get-- I walk across the Danube. And I sort of judge by the sun. The road sign said I had to go to Freiburg and then Pisek. You know, to Czechoslovakia. And it was, like, seven kilometers to Freiburg. And then Czechoslovakia is another 20 kilometers or something, up the hill.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I thought, you know, I'm on the other side of the Danube. I'm there. And I thought to myself, naively, I'm going to take a shortcut straight across country. And I knew it was the afternoon. You know, being a Boy Scout. The sun is in the west, so north is this way. And north was straight up the hill from the Danube. So I, instead of taking the road, I go straight up the hill. You see? And I hear shouting and firing.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So I sort of sit. And suddenly, I see a jeep coming towards me. And they are Russian POWs. So they stop. And they say, who are you? You know, what you doing here? And so on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Russians are sort of wonderful people to children. Really sort of-- they treat you with abandoned generosity. A young boy in Russian is patsan. And so they said, ah, patsan, you know you come with us. We'll look after you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I get into the jeep. You, Know this was sort of an adventure. And we go across country.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there were some other jeeps. There were a troop of about 20 men, all armed, you know, to the teeth, and sort of Russian uniforms. We are now in the Russian zone. And they are going from farm to farm. We get to a farm. And they say to me, you go to the farm and ask them, you know, for some bread. And then come back and tell us if there are any men around. You know, like I should spy out the land.

speaker: interviewee
text: So naively, I go. I come back and report. And then they attack the farm and rape the women and burn the farm. And I thought with all this shooting and this pillage, you know, having survived all this, this is not for me. So I-- the man in charge of them was a polkovnik. That's like a colonel in the Russian army. So he says, ah, you be our patsan, like a mascot. And we'll take you back to Russia. This is my second proposal.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'll take you back to our regiment. We'll look after you. We'll send you through university. You come with us. You've lost your parents.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, no, my father is in England. You know, I know how to get in touch with my father. I'm not coming to Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in fact, I'm going to Czechoslovakia. And they all laugh. You can't walk to Czechoslovakia. We're in jeeps. We'll take you. So they took me part of the way, north. You know, helter skelter, across country. You know, you can drive these jeeps anywhere. These American jeeps they had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take you to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Towards Freiburg. And they take me to a ridge. And they say, look, there is Freiburg. And we can see the city. Just walk towards the city, across the city, and you're back in your homeland. I say goodbye to this troop, thinking, you know, I'm well to be rid of them. And I walk down towards Freiburg. And I get, again, very thirsty and tired. And I see some milk churns by the roadside. So I tip them down. And I see there's some milk in the bottom of them. So I tip them down. And you know, if you tip a whole big milk churn, the milk comes at you suddenly. So you spill it all over your face. And I must have been weak and sort of already ill. Because I drank some of the milk. And then I just sat there. And suddenly, I see a truck coming towards me from Freiburg, with a flag, Russian flag. And it's green colored. And it's getting nearer and nearer. And I can see it's the fire engine from Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And who should be in the truck, but the fellow who I ran away from because I thought he'd have my ass? And he says, what you doing here, Michael? So I say, I'm walking to Czechoslovakia. And all the men burst out laughing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, as if-- it's crazy. They say, come back to Mauthausen. The Russians have a bus going every night back to Czechoslovakia. And we'll put you on the bus. And you'll be in Mauthausen. You'll be home tonight. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I didn't want to go back to Mauthausen. Whatever happened, I didn't want to go back to Mauthausen. But they said they wouldn't have it. You can't. That's crazy. You can't walk. Whoever heard of walking to Czechoslovakia? So they forced me into the truck and take me back to Mauthausen. And they prepare a clean bunk for me. And they put me through a shower.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they look at me and they say, but you-- you are completely lousy. So they stripped the whole bed. And they take all this stuff, put me through a sauna. And by now I had another pair of shoes. And the shoes come back completely ruined. And I've got a temperature. A doctor comes, American doctor. And they take me by jeep to a hospital. And three weeks later, I woke up after typhus. During the time that I was in hospital on the drips, I came to and told one of the nurses-- it was a male orderly-- that I had a father. And I told him this address in-- in Switzerland. And he wrote a postcard to Switzerland, that I am in hospital and that I'm recovering from typhus. And my father got that postcard. But he only knew the number of the hospital in Austria. My father, like crazy, went to the embassy, to the American embassy. And he showed them this. My son, you know, first news that I have about my son. And the American embassy said, we've got no way at all to find out where hospitals are, even of our forces. You know, that's the Army command. It's-- it's impossible to find out. We can't help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So you can imagine how my father felt. He knew I was alive. He knew I was in a hospital. Anyway, they get me well. And I have to repeat all this journey, back through Linz. And I now know the way. You know? And I'm going to walk back to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm caught by the Russians. And they say, no, no, you can't walk to Czechoslovakia. No way. You can't get a permit. We take you back to Mauthausen. And we'll evacuate you through Mauthausen. You got to do things by the book. So I was back in Mauthausen again, after this American hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you liberated this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was-- by this time it was June. Late June, in fact.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you make it back to Czechoslovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. So I-- I get back to Mauthausen. And they say, well, we're no longer sending buses directly into Bohemia. But you're from Moravia anyway. So there's a transport tomorrow, which goes on a ship on the Danube. And you're going to another clearing station in Wiener Neustadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew Wiener Neustadt is in the South of Austria. And I was sort of walking distance from Czechoslovakia. You see? I mean, Czechoslovakia has railways and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here I was, playing around with the Russians. The Russians wouldn't have it. Back you go to Wiener Neustadt. The journey took two-- two days, through Melk, which was another camp, by ship on the Danube. I mean, it was very nice. They treated us well, food and everything. And then by train to Wiener Neustadt, which is way-- you know, it's a hell of a journey back to Czechoslovakia from Wiener Neustadt. I arrive. Whenever I arrived somewhere, it was nighttime. I arrived nighttime, and there's a big party going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Cossacks are dancing the [NON-ENGLISH], you see? So I go and watch. And I ask, where does one register to go to Czechoslovakia? So they said, there's a bus leaving for Czechoslovakia in half an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is the bus. I go to the bus. The bus is full.

speaker: interviewee
text: And disconsolate, I walk towards the dancing. And who should I bump into? This polkovnik, this colonel. He is now in Wiener Neustadt, also being evacuated with his regiment back to-- so he says, come with us to Russia. What are you doing here? So I said, I fell ill and I got typhus. And I've just come out of hospital. And there's a bus that's leaving for Czechoslovakia. I wanted to get on it, and it's full. So he says, nonsense. He walks back to the bus with me. Talks to the driver. This is [? always, ?] sort of, people talk Russian and misunderstand each other. And the driver was Czech. You know? So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the driver says, plny. It's full. We haven't got room, even for a child. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So this polkovnik takes out a gun. And he says to the bus, is there anyone in the bus here who can drive a bus? And then two or three hands go up, you know? From inside the bus.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says to the driver, he says, look. You take this boy to Czechoslovakia, now. If you don't, I'm going to shoot you. And one of them is going to drive the bus. So I'll make room. I don't think he meant it. It was a joke. But it was quite serious. And the fellow said, well, all right. He can sit on the floor. So that night I was going back to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you very briefly tell me about what happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, up till now, I had-- I was sort of fighting, you know? I was going to get back. And my brothers were-- my two brothers were young men. So get home, ask where they are. Join up with my brothers. By this time, it was the beginning of July. Or-- or end of June, 1945, height of summer. And we traveled through Vienna. And I saw the bombed-- you know, the big wheel that they had on the [NON-ENGLISH]. Went over the only bridge that was-- that was still standing over the Danube. And we get to a place called Znojmo. And I took a train. And in-- it was lovely, getting into Czechoslovakia , and speaking to people in Czech again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you actually go back to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: All you had to do is show your number. You didn't have to pay for a ticket. I was in Valasske Mezirici the evening-- the next evening. And I arrived and walked to the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: They put me up for the night. And then they said, why don't you go to the [NON-ENGLISH]? That was-- I'm still friendly with Petra [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: He's my age. And he's half Jewish. The mother was Jewish. And we had left a lot of our things with the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So next morning, I went to the Yanzas. And the Yanzas bathed me. Then I went to the burgermeister, you know, the mayor, to get some special tickets so that I could buy a suit-- because I came in rags-- and shoes. So I bought some shoes with public money. They gave me money to buy. They gave me these special-- special coupons.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I bought myself a suit. Which, I'm not kidding you, was a wooden suit. It was a cloth where it was a woolen cloth. But in the cloth, there were bits of wood. And I came to England with this suit, this wooden-- wooden suit. Anyway, my brothers hadn't turned up in Valasske Mezirici. So I stayed overnight. And I took--

speaker: interviewer
text: Which-- which brothers were you expecting to turn up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Leib and Milush. They were the only two. Because the others were-- I wasn't expecting the brothers from Palestine, and the brothers from-- and the brother in England. I was expecting Leib and Milush.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were not in Valasske Mezirici. I took a train to [NON-ENGLISH]. You have to change. Because the Sudetenland train service was a different rail network.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans had it all figured out, you know, that they were going to take over the Sudetenland. So the Czech railway didn't go through [NON-ENGLISH]. And there was a connecting train to the southern station of [NON-ENGLISH], which was on the Czech Railways. But I get to a village, which I had walked to with my brothers during my childhood. I knew the place, well. A place called [NON-ENGLISH]. It's right on the crest. And that was the border between the Sudetenland and the German Reich, during the time when the Germans took over. And there was a train supposed to be in half an hour. But the engine had a failure. And there was no train till the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm going to stop the tape here.
